data.title: thyssenkrupp: Continued Outperformance Justifies Profit-Taking (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Whenever you invest in a company from undervaluation, or with reversal potential, one of the decisions you need to make is when you're going to take profits. You may decide, like me, to go for a certain price target ("PT") or a certain timeframe. The important thing, I argue, is to have some idea of what your targets are so that you're not guess-working when the company rises close to triple digits. In this article, I'm going to look at an excellent example of such a company in such a development.

thyssenkrupp AG (OTCPK:TKAMY, FWB: TKA) has been a disliked company by the market for many years, and this has been for very good reason. The company had fundamental issues and was in the midst of a very comprehensive restructuring plan, which more or less prevented good profits for the foreseeable future. However, the reason I invested was that I saw that the fundamental substance of ThyssenKrupp was being incredibly undervalued, even in the event of a multi-year recovery period.

That is why I bought - and that is why I, with a high conviction, went and stayed bullish, first in late 2024/early 2025, but also stayed with my bullish thesis back in March, despite calling profit-taking entirely possible at the time.

The return since the earlier article, and the return of my 0.4% position in TKAMY (at the time of purchase), is to date 176%, and for my article, above 170% in total. You can find that article here, and my last article here.

Since the last time, I am up another 30%+, which is 3x the S&P 500 (SPY) in the same time.

You may characterize this article as a "Victory lap," and after this development, I believe such an article is justified, especially given how many (or how few) analysts were positive on the stock.

ThyssenKrupp - The company's upside following a 172% return.

I've been through a number of short-term triple-digit returns. The one commonality is that once such highs are reached, a repeat of that is usually much harder. We can see that in ThyssenKrupp as well. Since peaking somewhat in April/May of this year, further development and reversal have been much slower. Several times I have been close to rotating, but I've held off until now.

Why?

Because the company recently hit €10/share for the native, which was one of my "exit" price targets.

Thyssenkrupp has long been in a position that I view as "out of danger." The combination of a very strong balance sheet with good management has taken care of that. For some time, the company has had a high net cash ratio, thanks to divestments. As of 1H25, which was reported about 1.5 months ago, the company is at a €4.4B net cash, with a liquidity of over €7B. While the company does not yet have a dividend, the dividend is a clear target for the company though I may not wait around for that to come.

For the last full-year period, the company generated sales of over €35B, with an EBIT of over half a billion. Not great numbers, but improving. The company remains a play on important basic materials. The three primary segments, especially with the geopolitical situation in Europe, are Materials, MX, Automotive, AT, and Marine, MS (MS is slated for disposal, however, which I'm not necessarily a fan of). Steel Europe is relevant as well, as it's the largest steel mill in Europe (integrated), but still unprofitable at this time.

The company has been moving towards its targets for years, with some of the segments close to or now hitting targets.

The 1H25/2Q25 period actually wasn't even that good. The company saw challenging macro trends, but the company's continued strategic improvements are paying dividends here. Also, the company received the cash from sales of the Indian steel segment, about €400M net of taxation. The next step in the major restructuring process is the Steel Europe restructuring, which requires union agreements and negotiations, with one of the largest unions in all of Europe, IG Metall, almost finished. The next step in terms of this process is a 50/50 JV that the company is building with EGP.

I still believe Marine system disposal to be a mistake, especially given the recent military trends, as well as new order extensions for two submarines. But the important part for Thyssenkrupp is the big picture.

As of 1H25, the company confirmed group guidance for the next period. The balance sheet is very strong, and the company has repaid its last outstanding bond, which means the company is basically debt-free at this point, which is an incredible and liberated position for both an individual and a company to be in. It's APEX 2 program seems by all indications on the bottom line, to be working.

We need to understand that current results trends are still impacted by weak markets and uncertainties on the macro level. Despite this, the company managed 1h25 sales on par or close to it, with the latest annual ones, with positive EBIT. It was less than I expected, but still very positive.

The company is far from out of its CapEx cycle, which is why the balance sheet is so cash-heavy. In 2024/2025 alone, CapEx is expected to increase by about half a billion on the top end, with increases from new construction costs for the DRI steel plant, as well as other growth investments.

I'm still somewhat mellow on the company's Decarbon technology segment. It's something the company wants to keep, and we'll see if it delivers alpha over time - but I personally like the legacy segments more, especially given the trends of tariffs, anti-dumping, and the like, which I believe is making ThyssenKrupp a stronger European choice over time. The company is one of the largest remaining, independent European materials distributors, and is significantly larger than most of its peers in this segment.

Overall, the thesis I have painted for Thyssenkrupp over the past year and more has materialized, and I believe the potential for further growth and appreciation is technically there. However, given my personal rate of return in this investment, I believe we're now at a level where I'm no longer willing to continue holding the business. At its heart, the company is a volatile basic materials business, with risks ranging from volatility (as we're seeing to wage risk, to supply chains, to similar ones. In addition, just because we're seeing a supportive geopolitical development, does not mean the company's operational and competitive risks, in the form of energy costs, competition from Asian Manufacturing, and a problematic Automotive, European automotive end market, are gone.

Thus, we need to temper our optimism, which most investors are prone to after a development like this, by weighing every positive argument with counterarguments. Even in my last article, I made it clear that rotation was a possibility. Now we're at a level where I say it may not just be a possibility, but for me, it has become a necessity.

Why?

Let's take a look.

thyssenkrupp - Risks are now outweighing upside, especially with the speculative part of the thesis

thyssenkrupp has always been a speculative investment, and thus part of the 7-8% or so in my portfolio that I focus on investments with a very high potential upside, but also a higher risk than I usually take.

Because I am a valuation investor, when looking at a position with a return like this, over 170% in total, I look at what I could do with the capital instead of keeping it in thyssenkrupp. An investment must "argue" for itself by having a continued upside that is above what I could get elsewhere.

Thyssenkrupp no longer does this. In my last article, I made a €12/share fair value price target clear, and while this target is still possible, I now believe that this company, for the time being, has run its course. Ideally, I'd want to invest below €6/share to get a significant discount to revenues and below a 10x P/E. I want a 10x NTM P/E or below, because the company is in such a major restructuring. The current forward revenue multiple, even now, is 0.1x, with a forward EBITD multiple of 2.22x. This highlights how volatile the market views the company to be, when peers like Nippon Steel (OTCPK:NPSCY) are at almost 0.6x revenues, a number that seems to have some market agreement, since both POSCO and JWS are between 0.6-2x. Reliance Steel (RS), as another peer, is at 1.2x, and typical EBITDA multiples for these range between 5-12x.

So, using these estimates, when the company is still significantly impacted, and current forecasts do not detail any sort of "quick" recovery, it means that the longer we hold, the higher risk we take.

Technically speaking, if we forecast at a 19-20x P/E, which is the company's 20-year average, we'd see fair value levels of €20-30 for the native ticker TKA, with potential RoR in total of 130% by 2027. But this is the most positive thesis I feel that I can publish and remain "serious" about the company's potential.

However, this thesis must in turn be tempered by the company's negatives. Over the past 10 years, it has never hit its targets, missing them 80-90% on the negative side. The company is BB-rated and lacks IG. Its dividend is very low, and its peers have better dividends. The positives are essentially zero debt and a restructuring that's going well. But if asked, I would give the company a sector-discounted valuation target of a fair value target of €12/share, which means my 15% annualized upside target is €9/share or below. And that's even if you're willing to take a speculative "leap" with a company like this.

I did not expect thyssenkrupp to, in fact, recover this quickly. And the return I'm seeing, I have companies in Europe in other segments which are not only A-rated or BBB rated, but with significant dividends, and much safer. Given the current state of geopolitics, that is something I prefer investing in here, and I am now taking profits for thyssenkrupp.

This investment has fulfilled its purpose.

Risks to my changed thesis are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The primary risk to my neutral/hold thesis and rotation is the materialization of the positive thesis you see above, with RoR of over 115%+. The thing is, I can get a rate of return like that more conservatively in any one of 15 European companies I currently have on my closer watchlist. Because thyssenkrupp always (since I invested in it), has been and continues to be a speculative play, I'm comfortable taking this capital out of "speculative" and into "conservative." Even when weighed against the risk of thesis materialization here, I don't see this as a risk big enough.

My updated thesis is as follows, and my €9/share PT comes to a TKAMY PT of $10.4/share, making this a "Hold."

Thesis

  • thyssenkrupp remains one of the more interesting global plays on steel, forging, and the steel sector in Europe. I now consider the company to be a rotation target, due to over 175% return in less than a year's time.
  • While thyssenkrupp could technically be undervalued on the basis of NAV, Peers, and forecasts, the company is now also with a heavy risk overhang of a correction following a massive surge. For that reason, I consider it more a speculative long-term play than a short-term one.
  • My fair value target for thyssenkrupp remains at €12/share, native, and my buy-in price target is somewhere along the €9/share mark. It should be noted that the company, however, is speculative at this time, and comes with that risk.
  • However, as of this time in July/August 2025, the company is a Hold.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which is not enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of €9/share share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a Hold rating as of July/August 2025. This is a speculative investment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Party Is (Nearly) Over For Live Nation Entertainment As Risks Mount
data.description:
Company Background

Live Nation Entertainment (NYSE:LYV) was founded in 2005. It progressed through an important merger in 2010 when it combined with Ticketmaster. The merger was not without its detractors, who feared for the music industry’s return to a monopoly position. Some high-profile artists publicly opposed the business combination, among them Bruce Springsteen, who said of the merger at the time:

‘The one thing that would make the current ticket situation even worse for the fan than it is now would be Ticketmaster and Live Nation coming up with a single system, thereby returning us to a near monopoly situation in music ticketing.’

Headquartered in Beverly Hills with Rapino now twenty years at the helm, LYV has become a behemoth in the industry, with an incredibly robust business model primed for sustained growth. Springsteen’s warning has become a reality, as the company have become, according to their own statements in the 2024 Annual Report:

  • ‘The largest live entertainment company in the world, connecting over 788 million fans across all of our concerts and ticketing platforms in 51 countries during 2024.’
  • ‘The largest producer of live music concerts in the world, based on total fans that attend Live Nation events as compared to events of other promoters, connecting 151 million fans to approximately 11,000 artists at 54,000 events in 2024.’
  • ‘One of the world’s leading artist management companies based on the number of artists represented.’
  • ‘The world’s leading live entertainment ticketing sales and marketing company, based on the number of tickets we sell … distributing over 637 million tickets through our systems in 2024.’
  • ‘One of the world’s largest music advertising networks for corporate brands’.

If their own estimates are true, which I believe they are, this means they control an outsized proportion of venues (394 as of April 2025) – including some of the most iconic – as well as the marketing, ticketing systems, and booking of those venues. Also, their investments in artists themselves – amounting to $14 billion in 2024 – positions them as a major controlling entity in terms of managing their performances.

Given the huge growth in live music, sports and entertainment attendance figures we have seen since the Covid trough, fuelled in part by a surplus of disposal income and a want for ‘experiences’ as part of what was initially termed ‘revenge spending’, and now increasingly fuelled by rising personal debt, it is no surprise that the company’s share price has been on a tear. Currently trading at a forward PE of 74, what investors want to know is, will growth be sustained, and do the shares represent good value. In this article, I’ll assess what I perceive to be the main opportunities and risks for the company, before assessing its finances and valuation.

Opportunities

After the Covid slump, figures from Statista suggest online ticket sales have not only returned to their 2019 levels but have far surpassed them.

This has been to LYV’s benefit, as revenue has bounced back strongly in these years.

In the TTM period, the company generated $22.74 billion, of which $18.63 billion was from concerts, $2.96 billion from ticketing, and $1.2 billion from sponsorship and advertising.

In terms of concerts, they have created a “flywheel” where they have come to own an increasingly large portion of the supply chain. They can direct artists toward their own venues, which they own and operate, selling merchandise and concessions to fans in the process, earn revenue through ticket sales through their own system (Ticketmaster), attract sponsors through their own industry connections, and advertise other artists and other venues that they own or operate.

They have a monopoly in the industry, and this represents an enormous competitive advantage over their rivals. By controlling more aspects of the supply chain, they can leverage this for cross-selling and brand promotion, recognize efficiency gains in terms of scaling, use their position of strength to outmuscle rivals when negotiating deals, and have much more leverage when the industry goes through downturns, such as during Covid. This flywheel effect positions them very well for future growth, especially as they seek to expand their presence internationally and into new markets like Japan, where they recently established a new relationship.

On these aspects alone, we can see that LYV has a strong moat. Moreover, they have a strong brand in Ticketmaster, the most recognizable name in the business of ticket sales for live entertainment. Their ticketing segment enjoys market dominance, with Ticketmaster enjoying a market share north of 60% according to Statista and other sources. Their dominance is reflected in the following chart:

There is much to like here, but there is also much to be cautious about. What are the key risks as I see them?

Risk 1: Slowing Growth

The first major risk is related to how the company is increasingly positioning itself in terms of its product/services offerings in the industry. Let us consider how we might conceptualize the business. Most obviously, it is not only seasonal (skewed toward the summer months) but also cyclical, based on consumer spending. Watching live entertainment is discretionary spending, so during market downturns this aspect of a person or family’s budget will necessarily decrease, thereby negatively impacting the revenue that LYV would make through ticket sales. With lessening demand, there might be a reduction in the number of artists touring, and artists might put on fewer shows or charge less for tickets to these shows. Indeed, while management stated that demand is solid, they did say supply was weakening in their latest earnings call. When asked about declining revenue, the FCO Joe Berchtold stated:

‘Everything that we've seen points to its supply, not – no demand issues, it's just less supply year-on-year. And when we were doing some of our forecasting, we were expecting consistent levels and therefore, the growth in Live Nation would have carried through to higher growth.’

The demand side could see some weakness too, and it seems there are signs of this. For fans themselves, not only are concerts discretionary, but they are also increasingly moving into purchases that we might describe as ‘luxury’, which are even more exposed to the forces of cyclicality when the key market is “regular people” rather than the wealthy. I would also suggest that the shift in their product mix is increasingly, if not inadvertently, moving them into the tourism industry as well, with many fans increasingly willing to travel out of state or country to watch a show. While this would almost certainly enhance the fan experience, it would also almost certainly limit how many shows that fans could attend in a given year. The want for a special experience has meant that fan volumes in the US will most likely fall in time given the rising costs associated with the experience. And this is especially likely as the venue mix is skewing more towards stadiums recently. Smaller venues are struggling in places like the US, UK, and Australia.

Anecdotally, we have all seen the prices rise to ever higher levels. Fewer people can go because of the huge expense, and logic suggests that prices can only rise so much before we see meaningful pull-back (churn). It would appear we are returning to a period of normal growth, as we saw in the decade before Covid. A lot of this can be anticipated by considering historical contexts.

The post-Covid environment has seen a lot of “revenge spending” on entertainment and experiences (cruises, tourism, live entertainment), for which LYV has benefitted, but the pendulum will eventually have to swing back toward careful spending amidst cyclical pullbacks, and a general return to normalized spending patterns, post-Covid. Moreover, the macro-economic picture is softening. Unemployment is slowly rising, interest rates are still elevated and there is no guarantee of respite from this in 2025 (especially with inflation staying meaningfully above 2%), and we have the impact of tariffs which is causing many businesses to freeze hiring and consolidate (hunker down) rather than grow. How will this turn out for businesses in the cyclical entertainment/tourism industry?

We can draw parallels with the 1920s, which commenced with hedonism after the end of the First World War, but soon led to the worst stock market crash in American history and a massive recession. The process took a decade to play out, and if this period is anything like the old one, we are approaching a critical period. What Americans didn’t have in the mid 1920s to accelerate this decline was sweeping tariffs on international trading partners. In fact, these did not come in until 1930 with the Smoot-Hawley tariffs, which – historians have tended to agree on – indeed accelerated and exacerbated the problems caused by the Wall Street crash and ensuing economic depression. We seem to be heading toward a similar path, yet with the stock market at all-time highs, we are ignoring the bigger picture risks. For companies that are particularly exposed to these cyclical threats, the party must end at some point—the question is when. The other question is: what are the specific signs to look for? Fan volumes, revenue, and tickets sold are the most important in my view.

Meaningful Declines in Fan Volumes, Revenue, and Tickets Sold

Here is a look at a comparison between North American and international fan numbers over the last eight quarters:

We are seeing a decline over the last four quarters in North America, the single most important market for the company. For LYV’s Q1 2025 results, internationally, growth in Latin America was stronger, with around a 25% year-on-year fan count, but increasing revenue here was not enough to offset the North American decline. Total revenue declined 11% year-on-year overall. While there was growth in sponsorship and advertising revenue, this represents a tiny portion of their overall sales. It is in concerts where a slight growth or decline can move the needle, and signs of slowing growth, if not decline, should be worrying for investors.

A look at revenue suggests LYV is already in hot water. Revenue has been in decline for three straight quarters, and I have no reason to believe this will not continue when the company reports Q2 earnings on August 7.

Much of this revenue growth previously enjoyed was fueled by what termed by S&P Global as “Funflation”:

‘Consumers have been willing to spend a greater proportion of disposable income on experiences rather than goods or savings, leading to funflation, or the bubble in pricing for live events.’

However, they anticipate moderating growth as consumers becoming increasingly ‘stretched’ and spending trends on discretionary items weaken. We are also seeing rising levels of credit card debt, as I mentioned earlier, particularly among the younger generations.

The S&P Global analysis stated their expectation for ‘entertainment spending growth to stabilize to 5%-10% in 2025’. Even if ticket inflation continues to increase the costs of live entertainment, and even if we see a slow shift toward stadium-based rather than amphitheatre-based concerts – the former being higher margin events – I do not believe this is enough to reverse the trend of declining revenue for LYV, moreover supports a PE of 74 or a P/OCF of over 17.

Fan volumes in the US are falling, and the same is happening in the UK, where recent research by Mintel has shown that over half of fans reduced their spending on live music events due to rising ticket prices. People are getting priced out in various markets.

Live Nation has recently announced plans to invest $1 billion to build new music venues in the US amidst what they perceive to be the current strong demand. Once these projects are complete, they expect to host up to 7 million more fans annually across 18 new venues. Is this likely? It would seem that they are making several assumptions to support their growth plans, which have a great chance of not panning out as expected. Firstly, they assume that demand for live entertainment will remain from a fan’s perspective. If consumers pull back on discretionary (and in some cases luxury) spending, this thesis will not play out. Secondly, is there enough supply of artists to make investments in new venues profitable? Indeed, the CFO mentioned this issue (falling supply) in the last earnings call. Thirdly, there is a possibility that new music venues will merely cannibalize existing offerings, making investments in this arena risky. Just as in professional sports, where the threat of cannibalization has prevented franchises being set up in certain locales (an NHL team in Hamilton, ON being an obvious example), there is also not an infinite number of music fans who could ensure a decent return on investment. The company would have to be very strategic in their construction targets, but as the largest cities already have large venues, is there necessary a market for another 18 of them to be built from scratch. This growth plan, to me, seems very risky. To pay for it, they would likely charge even higher prices for tickets, which would price out even more people. I just don’t think the demand is there, with live entertainment increasingly being pushed into the smaller, more niche luxury market. This is evident from the slowing growth in ticket sale numbers (not revenue from tickets per se, but rather the number of actual tickets sold):

I would not be surprised to see tickets sold growth dipping into the negative in the next few quarters. In Q1 2025, it was just 1.25%.

The business model posits a necessarily smaller number of fans spending an increasingly large amount of money for the live music experience, and the extent that Live Nation positions its business model around this idea was expressed, frankly, by the CFO, Joe Berchtold, in the last earnings call:

‘All these pieces matter, getting more fans into the Venue Nation buildings where we’re counting the beer money and the parking and the other pieces, also continuing to grow the per fan spend, all the various initiatives we have to make sure we’re catering to every fan from the traditional beer drinker to the person who wants non-alcoholic to the premium person. Doing everything we can to reduce friction and give everybody the best experience and extract as much money as possible from each person is going to continue to drive the economics of Venue Nation and then that will naturally flow through to help the margins in the concert business.’

In these words, I sense a disconnect here, if not a contradiction: to ‘give everybody the best experience and extract as much money as possible from each person’. Truthfully, the best experience would be an affordable one, wouldn’t it? Simply put, I just don’t see how this business model is sustainable during market downturns, which we could be approaching shortly. It is apparent that the market is not pricing this risk in at the stock’s current valuation.

Risk 2: Litigation

Another risk is related to ongoing litigation. I will not speculate on the cases, but instead present the facts and point readers toward the relevant documents so they can make their own conclusions.

On July 6, Juice Entertainment submitted a letter to the US Department of Justice and the FTC alleging Live Nation of several unlawful practices, which I would encourage prospective investors to read. This latter was submitted in response to President Trump’s Executive Order 14254: Combating Unfair Practices in the Live Entertainment Market (from Mar 31st 2025). I would also encourage all investors to read this EO and assess in particular its tone and tangential implications for LYV. Indeed, the threat of lawsuits against LYV has persisted for over 15 years but they have managed to escape relatively unscathed from any major litigatory consequences, though I note a recent class action settlement of $20 million as an exception.

Juice initially filed a lawsuit against LYV in 2010, relating to a case involving Juice’s involvement in running a New Jersey state fair concert event in 2011. Quoting the official record, the case alleged the latter ‘essentially prevented Plaintiffs [Juice Entertainment] from signing any artists for the concert by pressuring talent agencies not to work with Plaintiffs (Juice Entertainment]’. Live Nation sought a summary judgment, which was granted in 2018, but later appealed by Juice Entertainment, resulting in lengthy delays to the legal proceedings. While the EO does not necessarily or directly implicate Live Nation, it represents a clue as to where the eyes of the government are focused in relation to this industry, that is, towards the targeting of unscrupulous practices within the music and live entertainment industry that are ‘price-gouging consumers and depriving fans of the opportunity to see their favorite artists without incurring extraordinary expenses’.

This relatively small case is overshadowed by a much larger case against Live Nation Entertainment—a civil antitrust lawsuit filed by the Department of Justice and 30 state and district attorneys general in 2024 that alleges, again quoting from the official record: Live Nation ‘unlawfully maintained monopolies in several concert promotions and primary ticketing markets and engaged in other exclusionary conduct affecting live concert venues, including arenas and amphitheaters’. The complaint can be found here. In March, the company denied any wrongdoing and asked a federal judge in New York to dismiss the case, but this bid failed.

I do not feel the risk of a potential forced divestiture of Ticketmaster – a key component of its flywheel – is priced in here at all. According to a recent SWOT analysis by Investing.com, the consequences would include a weakening in their ‘negotiating power’ with venues and artists, thereby undermining its ability to ‘secure exclusive deals’ and ‘maintain its dominant market position’. I agree entirely.

The case remains ongoing, and I would urge investors to keep abreast of developments. For me, the litigation risks are enough to dissuade me from investing here, despite the company’s monopoly position. When I add this to what I perceive to be signs of slowing secular growth and the company’s overvaluation, I have even more confidence in my view on this one.

Financials and Valuation

Live Nation Entertainment has cash and equivalents of $7.16 billion, amidst current assets of $11.07 billion. It has current liabilities of $11.52 billion, but over $6 billion of this is ‘unearned revenue’, essentially deferred revenue (tickets purchased for events that have not happened yet). Of all the figures I could point to that seem to support the high share price, it would be this one. Management reinforced this point as well. However, it is important to remember that any cancellations or refunds being offered to fans would lower this figure. As such, this is not guaranteed income. It would be reasonable to assume that most of this would eventually become revenue, but it is also possible that people are purchasing concert tickets further in advance than they used to for fear that they might not be able to afford it later. This pushes up the deferred revenue figure but not necessarily the (realized) revenue figure down the road. Deferred revenue could drop over the next four quarters if my thesis is realized. This is a risk that investors should understand.

According to its latest 10-Q, LYV has a long-term debt figure of slightly over $5.9 billion, but has a total debt figure, net of unamortized debt discounts and issuance costs, of $6.5 billion. This is quite high for a young, cyclical company with a $35 billion market cap and less than $23 billion TTM revenue. Its TTM interest expense on this debt was over $325 million. At present, they generate ample FCF to cover this debt, but investors should also be aware that capex spending has not only meaningfully increased over the last few years but is set to rise still further, to an estimated $900 million to $1 billion for 2025, according to management, due to their new venue construction projects over the next 18 months. As such, declining FCF is something for investors to keep an eye on.

While gross margins have slightly increased in recent quarters, this has not offset the declines in other parts of their business.

In terms of valuation, LYV has a PE ratio of 51.56 and a forward PE of 74.34 according to Seeking Alpha. The last time their forward PE was this high was in 2022, when the company was growing like gangbusters amidst a return to huge discretional spending on leisure and entertainment, post-Covid. We are not in the same environment anymore. Meanwhile, their growth rates in terms of revenue and a host of other metrics as I discussed above are slowing if not dipping into the negative, hence the much higher forward earnings multiple. This gives them very poor valuation and growth grades from Seeking Alpha, which I would agree with.

Their current P/OCF is at an historical high of over 17, and any decline in OCF (highly likely) will see this multiple increase still further. Even if they can maintain their gross and net margins where they are, I do not see much upside here in terms of an upward multiple rerating. The risks are firmly outweighing the likely rewards in my view.

Conclusion

Due to the factors above, I rate Live Nation a sell. I view litigation risks as significant to their business, with a potential break up of the Live Nation/Ticketmaster combo on the cards, which would massively hurt their business model by breaking off a key component of their flywheel. This risk is amidst sky high valuations and slowing growth if not falling revenue in key segments of their business. I note that it might take several quarters for my thesis to play out, and until then shares might continue to rise. However, from a technical standpoint, shares are now trading near resistance, at just above $152, and I would expect some significant profit taking to occur if results for Q2 2025 do not hit a home run. Anything short of spectacular – i.e. a return to growth amidst recent decline – or any negative news regarding litigation from the DOJ, and I can see a major revision in earnings multiple. In my view, this is one of the poorest risk-reward profiles in the industry right now. It is a shame, as their business model has so much to warrant a buy rating – it has the kind of durable competitive advantages that I tend to look for in businesses – but I feel the party is over and the lights are about to turn on for LYV.

data.title: CrowdStrike: Nothing, Even Growth, Can Last Forever
data.description:
CrowdStrike Holdings (NASDAQ:CRWD) has soared alongside the rest of the tech sector as its software business proved resilient to tariff headwinds. The company maintains a net cash balance sheet and solid profit margins. The market looks overly focused on the near-term possibility of accelerating topline growth, which is a curious point given that the aggressive valuation would instead require a long-term view. I fail to make the math work here for market-beating returns. I reiterate my "Neutral" rating for the stock.

CRWD Stock Price

I last covered CRWD in April, where I recommended avoiding the stock due to valuation. The stock has since roared 27% higher.

The momentum is strong here, but I think the market is underestimating the potential downward pressure as growth inevitably slows down.

CRWD Stock Key Metrics

CRWD is a giant in the cybersecurity industry, first known for its endpoint protections but now known for having one of the most complete cybersecurity portfolios.

In the most recent quarter, CRWD generated 19.8% YoY revenue growth to $1.103 billion, coming in at the middle of guidance of between $1.1006 billion and $1.1064 billion. In spite of the macro environment, investors were likely hoping for CRWD to beat guidance as it typically does.

The company generated $201 million in non-GAAP operating income, crushing guidance of between $173.1 million and $180 million. While it is just one quarter, I should note that it is common for high-growth companies to eventually prioritize the bottom line (including guidance beats) as they mature and growth slows down.

CRWD ended the quarter with $4.6 billion of cash versus $744 million of debt, representing a strong net cash balance sheet. The company authorized a $1 billion share repurchase program, which is an interesting yet not quite unprecedented decision as other richly valued names like Snowflake (SNOW) have also been repurchasing shares.

Looking ahead, management guided for the company to see 19.5% YoY revenue growth in the second quarter and left the full-year guidance intact, which calls for 21.5% YoY growth.

On the conference call, management discussed their layoff of around 5% of their workforce in May, noting that they expect this to "add at least 1% to next year's non-GAAP operating margin from our previously discussed target," culminating in "at least 24%" margins in fiscal 2027 and a free cash flow margin of "more than 30%." Management expects adoption of new products to drive the implied acceleration in revenue growth in the second half of the year. We have seen Zscaler (ZS) successfully transition into a projected acceleration after its own first-half slowdown, so perhaps CRWD can do the same.

Is CRWD Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

As of recent prices, CRWD traded at around 24x sales. Consensus estimates call for the company to sustain 20% topline growth for many years.

That outlook looks too aggressive, in my view. The company has sustained aggressive growth rates in large part due to its wide product portfolio, which has allowed it to cross sell to existing customers. This is not a sustainable strategy long term as it works through its customer base.

Sure, one could make an argument that CRWD's products are top tier and mission critical, making it "inevitable" for successful cross selling. But again. It isn't clear just how much more juice is left in the tank. I expect that uncertainty to eventually proliferate in the stock price. Even if we assume that the company can meet aggressive consensus estimates, then the stock doesn't look cheap here. I can see the stock trading at around 10x sales in 2034, which might prove too aggressive given the likely drop off in growth rates. Based on 35% long-term net margins, that would equate to a 29x earnings multiple. The stock is priced for around 7.5% annual returns through 2034 assuming it hits consensus estimates and trades at a premium 10x sales multiple by then as well. Given the aggressive underlying assumptions, I view that return proposition as being woefully insufficient. I would be more interested in the name if the stock traded down to the 12x to 14x sales multiple range. While that would still represent a premium to the 6x to 8x multiples that I'm seeing in my coverage, this premium would be warranted given the more aggressive growth rates.

CRWD Stock Conclusion

CRWD, like many software names, has shown great resilience despite macropressures. Management has also implied an acceleration in topline growth exiting the year due to new product adoption. However, I highlight the underappreciated risk that the cross-selling strategy underpinning recent resilient growth rates will eventually fade as it saturates its customer base. I recommend investors search for cheaper tech names, as I have done for the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my "Neutral" rating for the stock.

data.title: Service Corporation Q2: As Steady As She Goes
data.description:
I assigned a Strong Buy rating to Service Corporation (NYSE:SCI) in April 2025, discussing its structural growth from aging populations. The company delivered a strong quarter with solid confidence in the growth in the second half of FY25. I reiterate a Strong Buy rating with a fair value of $101 per share.

Service Corporation released its Q2 FY25 on July 30, posting a 3.1% growth in comparable total funeral sales growth and 5.3% growth in cemetery preneed sales productions. As detailed in the table below, the company achieved 11% adj. EPS growth, a strong margin improvement during the quarter.

My key takeaway from the quarter was the management’s confidence in the second half of FY25. During the call, the management anticipated both preneed cemetery sales production and preneed funeral sales production will grow by low-to-mid single digit in the second half of FY25. Because of the strong performance, the company targets to achieve $880 million to $940 million in operating cash flow for FY25, with around $600 million in adjusted FCF. On an adjusted basis, the current stock price is trading at less than 18x free cash flow, a low level in my view.

Another highlight is the company’s share repurchase and generous capital return policy. YTD, the company has already repurchased $320 million in own shares, which is quite significant considering its total market capitalization of $10.7 billion. The company exited the quarter with a 3.68x net debt leverage, within its target range of 3.5 to 4x. As such, I think its strong balance sheet could support the company’s ongoing share repurchase activities. The total number of shares outstanding declined by 4.5% in FY23, followed by another 3.5% decrease in FY24. I estimate that the ongoing shares buyback will at least reduce its total number of shares by 3% annually in the near future.

As detailed in the slide below, Service Corporation is guiding for $3.85 in adj. EPS at the midpoint for FY25.

As discussed in my previous article, Service Corporation operates a very stable business model. I anticipate the company will deliver 3% revenue growth in FY25, considering its direct sales transition from trust to insurance model. As noted during the call, the company has already transitioned 95% of total productions, and anticipates the direct sales will return to growth in early FY26. As such, from FY26 onwards, I forecast the company will deliver 5% organic revenue growth. In addition, I anticipate the company will allocate 3% of revenue towards acquisitions in the funeral service market, contributing to an additional 1.3% to the topline growth.

I forecast the company will deliver 30bps annual margin expansion, driven by 10bps from pricing, and 20bps from operating leverage of SG&A. I calculate the normalized operating costs will grow by 5.8% annually, leading to an operating margin of 25.7% by FY34.

The DCF can be summarized as follows:

I calculate FCFF as below:

I revised WACC to be 8.7% assuming: risk free rate 4.2%; beta 0.9; equity risk premium 7%; cost of debt 5%; tax rate 25%; equity $11 billion; debt $4.8 billion. I set the terminal growth rate to be 4%, aligning with the overall market growth. With these assumptions, I calculate the total enterprise value to be $18.7 billion in total and the fair value to be $101 per share after adjusting net cash and debt positions.

Key Risk

In the U.S. market, many Hispanic and Asian customers prefer cemetery services. Due to the Trump administration’s immigration policy, I think some Hispanic and Asian populations might leave the U.S. If that occurs, the population mix change could pose some growth challenges to Service Corporation.

In addition, the gross margin of its cemetery business declined by 110bps during the quarter, caused by higher sales compensations. Investors need to closely monitor the company’s gross margin in upcoming quarters to ensure these elevated sales compensation payments do not become a persistent issue.

Conclusion

Service Corporation delivered very solid second quarter results, and the management remains confident in its core production growth for the second half of FY25. I think its stock price is undervalued and reiterate a Strong Buy rating with a fair value of $101 per share.

data.title: Axos Financial: Solid Growth, But Wait To Pull The Trigger
data.description:
Today we check in on and cover the just-reported earnings Axos Financial (NYSE:AX) One thing to note unlike many other banking institutions we cover, there is no dividend here. Instead the company reinvests for growth. Thus far here in this quarter, our coverage of financials and regional banks, which we consider 'pulse-checks' on local economies, indicates the economy has been resilient as a whole. There have been little to no signs of fiscal stress despite risks from tariffs, and somewhat softening economic data. While performance varies depending on the bank, the sector has been strong. Overall, the key metrics have held up very well. is the holding company for Axos Bank, Axos Clearing LLC and Axos Invest, Inc. Axos Bank is provides consumer and business banking products nationwide, and also provides comprehensive securities clearing services to introducing broker-dealers and registered investment advisor correspondents, as well as digital investment advisory services to retail investors. So, it is a bit more specialized than the average consumer focused bank, but is a strong performer. Let us discuss.

Axos Bank headline results and net interest income

Axos saw revenue of $321.5 million, which was a 10.5% increase in this critical metric year-over-year, and beat estimates by $9.2 million. What about margins? We saw strength here as well. Net interest margin rose from the sequential Q3 by 6 basis points to 4.84%, which is incredibly strong, and above average in the banking sector based on our universe of coverage. Net interest income and non-interest income increased by $4.7 million and $7.9 million, respectively, on a linked quarter basis, to $276.7 million and $256.5 million, respectively. This is strong.

We should also note that the provision for credit losses was $15 million, mostly reflecting the new assets held and a reserve built out. We monitor this metric from this current level as provisions do offset earnings power, and the provisions were up from $6 million a year ago. For the fiscal year provisions were $55.8 million up from $32.5 million. EPS (adjusted) for the quarter was a very strong $1.94, reflecting growth in loans, and strong margins. These were up from $1.83 in the comparable year ago quarter, and for the fiscal year, EPS was $7.50, up from $6.74.

Axos Bank's loans, deposits, and asset quality

We always monitor trends in loans, deposits, and key asset quality metrics. Loans were up $855 million sequentially. Loans, net of credit loss allowances, finished the quarter at $21.05 billion, a huge increase from $19.23 billion a year ago. It was a tremendous fiscal year.

Given the huge increase in the loan book, the allowance for credit losses rose from last year. These allowances were up to $290.05 million, which was up from $260.54 to start the year.

A more meaningful metric is the allowance for credit losses as a percentage of all loans: this was 1.36% compared to 1.34% to start the year. This is a bump, which suggests additional allowances are being made on both the back of higher loans, and perhaps some risk of non-payment associated with select loans. Net charge-offs were 0.16% of average loans, up from 0.05% a year ago. Thus, we do see some softening of asset quality, and this is something that needs to be monitored.

We can also still look at the return metrics to understand the earnings power here. We saw more loans and higher margins which is strong. The return on average assets was up 4 basis points from the year ago quarter to 1.85%. However for the fiscal year, returns on average assets were down 26 basis points to 1.82%, from 2.08%. This is a negative. We saw a weaker return on equity of 16.85% in the quarter, down from 18.81% a year ago, while for the fiscal year, this metrics was down 434 basis points to 17.30%. For the fiscal year we also saw less efficiency. That said, the efficiency of the bank is better than the average bank we cover, hitting 46.84%, compared to 43.59% in the prior fiscal year.

Stock is expensive relative to book value, but book value is expanding

We love to buy bank stocks when the key metrics are sound when share prices are close to book or even tangible book. In today's market, this is a rare find. Axos trades at a significant premium, but you are paying for the growth. Shares are at $88 at the time of this writing, and that is a huge premium-to-book value, which was $47.46 to end the quarter. However, this is up from $40.26 to start the fiscal year. Tangible book was up to $44.60 from $37.26 a year ago. Still, this is a significant premium.

Final thoughts

This is a growing bank, with some mixed key metric performance. We are not concerned with the softening of asset quality metrics, though these need to be monitored. But we think it is best to wait for a broader market pull back before entering shares, because there is a significant premium relative to book value. While book value, and as such the true value of the bank is growing, following loans, revenues, and earnings power, higher, we think it is best to wait.
